Course Title: AI-Generated Scams in Travel and Hospitality
Category: Hospitality & Tourism: Hotels, Tourism, Travel Agencies, Food & Beverage
Description: Fake bookings, phishing, and impersonation risks.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with everyday internet use and a general awareness of how travel bookings are made online is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify common AI-generated scams targeting the travel and hospitality sector, including fake bookings, phishing attempts, and impersonation schemes. You will also be able to explain how these threats operate and apply practical strategies to protect your business and customers from falling victim to them.

lessons.lesson title: AI-Generated Scams in Travel and Hospitality
lessons.Description: Artificial intelligence is making it easier than ever for scammers to create convincing fake travel websites, fraudulent booking confirmations, and highly personalised phishing emails that are difficult to spot. This lesson introduces you to the most common AI-generated scams targeting travellers and hospitality businesses, including fake hotel listings, impersonation of well-known travel brands, and deceptive customer service chatbots. You will learn how these scams are constructed, what warning signs to look out for, and how to protect yourself and your organisation from falling victim. The content is designed for beginners, so no prior knowledge of artificial intelligence or cybersecurity is required. By the end of this lesson, you will feel more confident identifying suspicious activity and taking practical steps to stay safe when booking or managing travel.
lessons.content: Fake bookings, phishing, and impersonation risks.

slides.subtitle: Understanding AI-generated travel scams
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools are now being used to create convincing fake travel deals and fraudulent booking sites
• Scammers target hotels, airlines, and holiday rentals
• Phishing emails and fake customer service accounts are increasingly hard to spot
• Real losses, real victims
• This course gives you practical skills to recognise and avoid these threats before they cause harm

slides.slidetitle: Why AI Makes Scams More Dangerous
slides.subtitle: The technology behind modern travel fraud
slides.slidecontent:
• AI creates professional-looking fake websites in minutes, not hours
• Personalised phishing messages can be generated at massive scale
• Even experienced travellers are being deceived by realistic content
• Technology advances faster than public awareness of risks
• Traditional warning signs may no longer apply

slides.subtitle: Red flags for hospitality businesses
slides.slidecontent:
• Requests from free email addresses instead of business domains
• Pressure tactics demanding immediate confirmation
• Unusual payment methods like wire transfers or gift cards
• Oddly formal language or subtle spelling errors
• Missing verifiable contact details or company addresses
• Booking details that don't match your standard procedures

slides.subtitle: How fraudsters exploit artificial intelligence
slides.slidecontent:
• Instant fake hotel websites with stolen photos and fabricated reviews
• Perfect clones of legitimate booking confirmation emails
• AI chatbots that convincingly impersonate travel agents
• Deepfake voice calls mimicking real customer service representatives
• Sophisticated phishing links embedded in attractive travel deals

slides.subtitle: Questions to ask before you book
slides.slidecontent:
• Compare prices across multiple legitimate booking platforms
• Research the company's physical address and registration details
• Look for secure payment options, not transfers or gift cards
• Verify customer reviews on independent review sites
• Be cautious of unsolicited offers via email or social media

slides.subtitle: What to verify before entering payment details
slides.slidecontent:
• Confirm the website URL starts with HTTPS, especially on payment pages
• Check for professional website design and functioning contact forms
• Look for recognised security badges and payment processor logos
• Verify the site has a clear privacy policy and terms of service
• Test customer service contact methods before committing to purchase

slides.subtitle: Practical steps for ongoing protection
slides.slidecontent:
• Always confirm bookings directly with service providers
• Use official company websites rather than third-party links
• Enable two-factor authentication on travel and payment accounts
• Set up account alerts for unusual activity
• Keep software and browsers updated with latest security patches

slides.subtitle: Your role in fighting travel fraud
slides.slidecontent:
• Report suspicious websites and messages to relevant authorities
• Alert your bank immediately if you suspect fraud
• Share scam warnings with colleagues and customers
• Document evidence of attempted fraud for law enforcement
• Stay informed about emerging scam techniques and warning signs

scripts.script: Welcome, and thank you for joining this course. We're going to look at something that's becoming a very real problem for travellers and hospitality businesses alike. Artificial intelligence has made it easier than ever for scammers to create fake travel deals, fraudulent booking websites, and phishing emails that look almost identical to the real thing. Hotels, airlines, holiday rentals—none of them are off limits. The consequences are serious. We're talking about real people losing real money, sometimes significant amounts. This course is designed to give you practical, straightforward skills to help you spot these threats before they do any damage. You don't need a technical background to follow along. Everything we cover is accessible and immediately useful.

Let's talk about why this is such a pressing issue right now. Not long ago, creating a convincing fake travel website took real skill and effort. That's simply no longer the case. AI tools have made it remarkably cheap and quick for scammers to produce fake hotel listings and fraudulent airline deals that look completely legitimate. These tools also allow scammers to personalise their messages, so phishing emails no longer feel generic—they can feel like they were written specifically for you. The result? Even seasoned, experienced travellers are being caught out. The technology is genuinely moving faster than most people's awareness of the risks, which is exactly why understanding this matters so much.

How do you spot a fake booking request before it causes a problem? First, check where the email is coming from. Legitimate businesses use their own domain, so if it's coming from Gmail or Hotmail, that's a red flag. Watch out for pressure tactics where someone is pushing you to confirm quickly without giving you time to think. Be cautious if they're asking for unusual payment methods like wire transfers or gift cards. The language can also give things away—it might be oddly formal or just feel off. If there are no verifiable contact details or a proper company address, that's another warning sign. And if the booking details don't match your usual forms or systems, trust your instincts.

Let's examine the specific tactics that scammers are using, and some of these are quite alarming. With AI, fraudsters can create a convincing fake hotel website in minutes, complete with professional photos and reviews. They can clone real confirmation emails so precisely that you'd struggle to tell the difference from the genuine article. AI-powered chatbots pretend to be travel agents, answering questions fluently and building trust before taking your money. Some scammers now use deepfake technology to impersonate customer service representatives in phone calls, with voices that sound completely real. Each of these tactics is designed to exploit your trust, so awareness really is your first line of defence.

When evaluating travel offers, start by comparing prices across multiple legitimate booking platforms. If one deal is dramatically cheaper than everything else, that's a serious red flag. Research the company properly—look up their physical address, phone number, and registration details. If you can't find any of that information, walk away. Pay attention to payment methods too. Requests for bank transfers or gift cards are huge warning signs, since those payments are almost impossible to recover. Be especially cautious about unsolicited offers arriving by email or social media, even if the account looks familiar, because scammers are very good at impersonating legitimate brands.

Before you enter any payment details, there are crucial technical checks to perform. Always verify the website URL starts with HTTPS, especially on payment pages—this shows the connection is encrypted. Look for professional website design and test whether contact forms actually work. Check for recognised security badges and legitimate payment processor logos. Make sure the site has a clear privacy policy and terms of service, as legitimate companies are legally required to provide these. If possible, test their customer service contact methods before making any purchase. These technical indicators can reveal a lot about whether you're dealing with a genuine business.

Building effective protection requires ongoing habits, not just one-time checks. Always confirm your bookings directly with hotels, airlines, or tour operators, rather than relying solely on third-party confirmations. When booking online, navigate to official websites yourself instead of clicking links in emails or ads. Enable two-factor authentication on your travel accounts and payment apps—it adds a simple but powerful extra layer of security. Set up account alerts so you're notified of unusual activity. And keep your software and browsers updated, as security patches protect against the latest threats scammers are using.

Finally, remember that fighting scams is a community effort. If you encounter suspicious websites or messages, report them to the relevant authorities—most platforms have straightforward reporting processes. If you suspect you've been targeted by fraud, alert your bank immediately. Share scam warnings with colleagues, customers, and family members, because spreading awareness helps protect others. Document any evidence of attempted fraud, as this can be valuable for law enforcement. Stay informed about emerging scam techniques, because as AI technology evolves, so do the methods fraudsters use. Your vigilance today helps create a safer environment for everyone who travels.

Who is this for: Hotel managers, travel agents, tourism operators, restaurant managers, hospitality staff, customer service representatives, front desk agents, reservation staff, booking coordinators
Team or Dept this is for: Hospitality Operations, Customer Service, Reservations, Sales, Marketing, Front Office, Guest Relations
